title: SEGMENT 16 — Liquid biopsy: tracking cancer from a blood draw

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Liquid biopsy: tracking cancer from a blood draw
I want to spend a quick segment on a technology that, over the last decade, has quietly changed how we monitor cancer.
It's called liquid biopsy, and the central idea is straightforward.
Cancer cells, like all cells, die regularly.
When they die, they release fragments of their DNA into the bloodstream.
Those fragments are called circulating tumor DNA, or ctDNA, and they swim around in your blood plasma, mixed in with the DNA from your normal dying cells.

If you draw a tube of blood from a cancer patient and extract the cell-free DNA from it, somewhere between a tiny fraction and a substantial fraction of that DNA, depending on tumor burden, came from cancer cells.
And — this is the powerful part — that ctDNA carries the cancer's mutations.
If the cancer has a KRAS G12C mutation, you can find that mutation in the blood.
If the cancer has acquired a T790M resistance mutation, you can detect it in the blood weeks or months before the cancer becomes large enough to be visible on a scan.
The analogy I like: imagine you can't see what's happening in a city, but you have an analyst monitoring everything that flows out through the sewer system.
You don't see the city directly, but you see what it's leaving behind.
The composition of the discharge tells you what's happening inside.
Liquid biopsy is finding a few different uses.
Genotyping when you can't get a tissue biopsy.
Sometimes a tumor is in a place that's hard or risky to biopsy — deep in the chest, near a major vessel.
Sequencing the ctDNA from blood gives you the cancer's genetic profile without a needle going anywhere near the tumor.
Monitoring response.
When a drug starts working, ctDNA levels drop, sometimes within days.
When the drug stops working, ctDNA levels start rising again, often before scans show anything.
You get an early warning that the cancer is regaining ground.
Detecting resistance mutations early.
When a cancer is evolving toward resistance, the resistant clone may make up only one percent of the tumor for a while.
A tissue biopsy might miss it.
But sensitive sequencing of ctDNA can pick up rare mutations, alerting the oncologist that resistance is brewing in time to switch strategies.
Minimal residual disease, MRD.
After a curative-intent treatment like surgery for colon cancer, the question is whether any cancer cells are left.
Standard imaging can't see small residual disease.
But ctDNA can.
If a patient is ctDNA-positive after surgery, they have residual cancer somewhere and are highly likely to relapse without further treatment.
If they're ctDNA-negative, they're much more likely to be cured.
This lets us escalate or de-escalate adjuvant therapy intelligently rather than treating everyone the same.
Multi-cancer early detection.
This is the most ambitious application and still proving itself.

The idea is that small, undiagnosed cancers anywhere in the body might be detectable by their ctDNA in a routine blood draw — and not just detected, but localized to a tissue of origin, based on the methylation pattern of the DNA fragments.
Tests like Galleri are starting to come to market based on this principle.
Whether they actually catch enough cancers early enough to save lives, without too many false positives, is being figured out in large ongoing trials.
The arc is clear, though.
We used to need a chunk of tumor tissue to know anything molecular about a cancer.
Now, increasingly, we need a tube of blood.
The implications for monitoring, for early detection, and for the experience of being a cancer patient are significant.
